segments.title: Insect class China and Tigris origins
segments.summary: The Insect class is introduced as the Royal Navy's large China gunboat type, with roots in river campaigns and imperial presence work.
segments.body: This watch point explains why the Insect class should be read as a river and station vessel rather than a light cruiser despite its six-inch guns. Dr. Clarke links the class to the Fly-class small China gunboats, the Tigris River campaign, and earlier lessons about moving goods and firepower along rivers. The segment gives the basic operating logic: a navy needed shallow-draft armed ships where inland waterways, treaty-port crises, and expeditionary campaigns made larger warships irrelevant.

evidence.note: The transcript calls the Insect class the large China gunboats and places the Fly class beside them as small China or Tigris gunboats.
The transcript ties the ships to the Tigris and Mesopotamia campaigns, river movement of goods, and gunboat presence in China.

segments.title: Treaty-exempt gunboat design fit
segments.summary: The class fits the treaty-era small-ship category: under 2,000 tons, slow, torpedo-free, lightly protected, and modified as threats changed.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke uses the Insect class to show why small gunboats sat outside the headline treaty debates. Their displacement, speed, and lack of torpedoes kept them in the same broad exemption space as sloops, even though their six-inch guns gave them real local punch. The design tradeoff is plain: little or no formal armor, improvised protection where possible, and later anti-aircraft additions for ships that were expected to work close to shore rather than fight in a battle line.

evidence.note: The transcript says gunboats fit with sloops because they are under 2,000 tons, below 20 knots, and carry no torpedoes.
The transcript describes wartime weapon changes, lack of armor, improvised protection, and 1939 replacement of some six-inch guns.
The transcript describes distant-station presence, close-inshore access, and the ships being unsuitable for rough seas.

segments.title: Mesopotamia mobile fire support
segments.summary: The Mesopotamia campaign shows why Insect and Fly gunboats mattered: they gave mobile firepower and river access that land artillery could not provide.
segments.body: This segment moves from the 1914 Basra landing and halted advance to the moment when gunboats made further river movement more practical. Dr. Clarke stresses that the advance toward Kut and Baghdad depended on Insect and Fly-class gunboats for fire support. The key lesson is operational rather than decorative: in river campaigns, a gunboat could move with the front and bring guns to bear where towed artillery could not keep pace.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the initial Mesopotamia campaign, the Basra landing, and the difficulty of moving north toward Baghdad.
The transcript says the Insect and Fly-class gunboats supported river movement and that advances depended on their mobile fire support.
The transcript summarizes their fire-support purpose and caveats their accuracy while moving.

segments.title: Wanhsien crisis and gunboat diplomacy
segments.summary: The 1926 Wanhsien incident turns the Insect-class gunboat into a crisis-management tool, where presence, coercion, and risk all meet.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke's account of the Wanhsien incident shows what gunboat diplomacy looked like when it stopped being symbolic. HMS Cockchafer intervened after Chinese troops seized British merchant shipping, and the later British rescue effort used an armed merchant ship, naval crews, and covering fire under hostile conditions. The episode is useful because it shows both sides of the tool: a small gunboat could project immediate force far inland, but once the bluff was called the situation could turn into a costly firefight.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces HMS Cockchafer in the 1926 Wanhsien incident and describes the seizure of a British merchant ship and the gunboat-backed intervention.
The transcript describes the armed rescue attempt, shore fire, boarding under fire, casualties, and return fire.
The transcript frames the incident as the problem with gunboat diplomacy when bluff becomes fighting and notes the pressure on General Yang afterward.

segments.title: HMS Cricket's Mediterranean loss and surviving wreck
segments.summary: HMS Cricket's final service traces an old river gunboat from China to Mediterranean inshore duty, air attack, and a surviving wreck off Cyprus.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke follows HMS Cricket from China to the Mediterranean Inshore Squadron, where Italian aircraft crippled her in 1941. Declared a constructive total loss, she was stripped for spares and her hulk was later used as an RAF target off Cyprus. The surviving wreck gives the class a tangible archaeological afterlife and shows how vulnerable these shallow-draft gunboats became once exposed to modern air power.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Cricket's transfer from China to the Mediterranean, her crippling by Italian air attack, and her declaration as a constructive total loss.
The transcript says Cricket's hulk was towed to Cyprus, used for RAF target training, and remains accessible to divers.

segments.title: Panay and Yangtze crisis risk
segments.summary: British Insect-class ships around the Panay incident show how river gunboats sat inside 1930s China crisis politics.
segments.body: The Panay section connects British and American river patrol work on the Yangtze. Dr. Clarke notes that Ladybird and Bee were attacked as well, helped pick up survivors, and operated in a crowded information environment with journalists and foreign-owned vessels close to the fighting. The watch point helps explain why gunboats were not just patrol craft: they were diplomatic signals, rescue platforms, and potential flashpoints in the same narrow river space.

evidence.note: The transcript says Ladybird and Bee were also attacked, reached the scene to pick up survivors, and that Japan accepted responsibility and apologized.
The transcript describes the attack on USS Panay, machine-gunning of small boats, and the American flag painted on the ship.
The transcript explains why journalists and other observers used American or British gunboats to get close to fighting while retaining some protection.

segments.title: Wartime Insect-class support in the Mediterranean
segments.summary: The later ship-by-ship survey shows Insect-class gunboats shifting from China service into Mediterranean fire support, survival, and improvised utility.
segments.body: This watch point follows several ships into their Second World War afterlives. Tarantula becomes a support and office ship, Moth is scuttled and later recovered by Japan, Ladybird is stripped down for movement to the Mediterranean, and Gnat survives a torpedo hit to become a fixed anti-aircraft platform. Dr. Clarke then draws the operational point: these old, shallow-draft ships remained valuable because they could get close inshore and deliver six-inch gunfire for landing and support operations.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Moth, Tarantula, repair/support use, and Tarantula serving as Bruce Fraser's British Pacific Fleet flagship.
The transcript follows Ladybird from Singapore toward the Mediterranean, Bardia, Kastellorizo, Tobruk support, and damage by dive bombers.
The transcript describes Gnat supporting Tobruk, surviving a U-79 torpedo hit, serving as an anti-aircraft platform, and summarizes the class's fire-support value.

segments.title: HMS Gnat at Nanking and the next gunboat generation
segments.summary: HMS Gnat's Nanking service and relief by HMS Grasshopper connect interwar protection duties with the Royal Navy's newer Dragonfly-class gunboats.
segments.body: The Nanking incident shows HMS Gnat performing the protective side of gunboat diplomacy by helping safeguard British and other international civilians during unrest. Her later relief by HMS Grasshopper introduces the Dragonfly class, evidence that the Royal Navy still saw a need for purpose-built river gunboats in the late 1930s. The transition links the Insect class's imperial station work to the next generation of small inshore vessels rather than treating the type as a First World War dead end.

evidence.note: The transcript says Gnat participated in the Nanking incident and helped protect British and other international citizens in China.
The transcript says Gnat was relieved by HMS Grasshopper and identifies the six newer Dragonfly-class gunboats and their four-inch armament.

segments.title: Gunboats as force multipliers
segments.summary: The closing argument treats gunboats and sloops as treaty-light force multipliers that could have freed destroyers for fleet work.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke uses the scrapping of older Insect-class ships and the late Dragonfly-class program to make a procurement argument. He argues that treaty limits did not prevent Britain from building useful gunboats and sloops in greater numbers before the Second World War. The broader force-structure lesson is that small, unglamorous vessels could take on inshore, river, and anti-submarine tasks, freeing destroyers and larger combatants for missions only they could perform.

evidence.note: The transcript says some Insect-class ships had been scrapped and argues treaties still allowed categories such as sloops and gunboats to be built in useful numbers.
The transcript discusses late Dragonfly-class construction and losses, showing how more small ships might have mattered.
The transcript argues for regular peacetime gunboat and sloop construction, freeing destroyers and treating small ships as force multipliers.
